ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Xinjiang stir-fried rice noodles. FOODON_00002187 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Xinjiang-style stir-fried rice noodles. A00LC (noodles, stir-fried) is used as the closest parent category within the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Xinjiang stir-fried rice noodles as a specific food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Xinjiang stir-fried rice noodles is approximately 350 g, typically served as a single-dish meal on a plate.

nutrient_source.title: Stir-fried rice noodles with meat and vegetables — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite estimate adapted for Xinjiang-style preparation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy and Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Xinjiang stir-fried rice noodles exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked rice noodles (FDC ID 168930), stir-fried beef (FDC ID 174032), and stir-fried vegetables, weighted by typical Xinjiang-style recipe proportions (approximately 55% rice noodles, 25% meat, 20% vegetables and sauce). Cumin and chili seasoning contribute negligible macronutrient change at typical quantities. The resulting composite per-100g values reflect a moderately energy-dense stir-fried noodle dish with moderate protein from lamb/beef and moderate fat from wok oil.

gi_source.title: Rice noodles, cooked — Glycemic Index Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Xinjiang stir-fried rice noodles. The GI of 53 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked rice noodles (GI approximately 53, low category), which forms the dominant carbohydrate component (~55% by weight) of this dish. Stir-frying with protein (lamb/beef) and vegetables further attenuates the glycemic response relative to plain boiled rice noodles. GL per serving calculated as: (53 × 21.5 × 350 / 100) / 100 = 39.9 g. This is a high glycemic load per serving due to the large portion size, consistent with a full meal plate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1470 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning, which can significantly contribute to daily sodium intake exceeding the recommended 2000 mg/day limit in a single serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving despite low-GI rice noodle base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the glycemic index of rice noodles is low (GI ~53), the large serving size (350 g) results in a high glycemic load (~40 g), which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to moderate the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and high sodium from wok-fried meat and soy sauce seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains moderate saturated fat (~1.6 g per 100 g) from lamb or beef and wok oil, combined with high sodium (~420 mg per 100 g), both of which are risk factors for cardiovascular disease; individuals with CVD should request reduced oil and reduced-sodium soy sauce preparation and limit portion size.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Xinjiang Stir-fried Rice Noodles (新疆炒米粉, Xīnjiāng chǎo mǐfěn) is a Northwest Chinese wok-fried noodle dish originating from the Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region of China. The dish features thin rice noodles (mǐfěn) stir-fried at high heat in a wok with sliced lamb or beef, bell peppers, onion, tomato, garlic, and a bold spice blend dominated by cumin and dried chili — hallmark flavours of Xinjiang cuisine that reflect Central Asian culinary influences. The noodles are seasoned with soy sauce and sometimes a touch of dark soy for colour, resulting in a savoury, smoky, mildly spicy dish with a chewy-tender noodle texture and caramelised meat. In Singapore, this dish is served at Xinjiang and Northwest Chinese specialty restaurants such as Bei Jiang Restaurant, typically as a single-plate meal priced around SGD 18. Nutritionally, a 350 g restaurant serving provides approximately 567 kcal, 27 g protein, 18 g fat, and 75 g carbohydrate. The rice noodle base has a low glycemic index (~53) but the large portion size results in a high glycemic load (~40), making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is high (~1470 mg per serving) due to soy sauce and seasoning, which is a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally dairy-free and typically halal when prepared with lamb or beef. It is not suitable for individuals with gluten sensitivity due to soy sauce. The cumin-forward spice profile and rice noodle base distinguish this dish from Cantonese or Hokkien stir-fried noodle preparations.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Xinjiang stir-fried rice noodles was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for component ingredients (cooked rice noodles, stir-fried beef/lamb, stir-fried vegetables) weighted by typical recipe proportions. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked rice noodles as the dominant carbohydrate source. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data; no single approved-source entry for this specific dish was identified. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimate. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimate. GI is an analogous estimate based on cooked rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database (GI ~53, low category); no direct GI measurement for Xinjiang stir-fried rice noodles was found in the approved GI literature. Sodium content is high due to soy sauce seasoning typical of this dish style. The dish is typically prepared with lamb in authentic Xinjiang style but beef is commonly substituted in Singapore restaurant versions. Halal status reflects typical lamb/beef preparation; individual restaurant sourcing should be verified. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and carry medium confidence.
